channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Renters ripped off? Shock report exposes rule-breaking letting agents
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Nearly half of UK private renters have encountered letting agents breaking industry rules over the past three years, according to new research from Citizens Advice. The findings reveal widespread issues ranging from delayed emergency repairs to unlawful fees, leaving millions of tenants facing substandard service amidst record rental prices.

Widespread problems in private rentals

The charity's study suggests approximately four million renters may have experienced practices that contravene regulations. Among the most concerning findings, 68% of tenants reporting emergencies like gas leaks or electrical faults waited over 24 hours for action, with 29% stating urgent repairs were never completed. The research also uncovered instances where agents improperly pushed zero-deposit schemes or charged prohibited inventory fees.

Deposit return times emerged as another significant issue, with more than half of tenants waiting beyond the legal 10-day period to recover some or all of their deposit. These problems persist despite existing protections under the Tenant Fees Act 2019, which banned most letting fees in England and introduced deposit caps.

Renters' Reform Bill under scrutiny

Citizens Advice warns these systemic failures could undermine the government's forthcoming Renters' Reform Bill unless accompanied by stronger enforcement. The charity argues that without proper regulation and accountability, the legislation may fail to deliver meaningful improvements for tenants already struggling with affordability pressures.

Tom MacInnes, Citizens Advice's Director of Policy, commented: "Private renters are paying record amounts yet receiving substandard service. No tenant should face dangerous living conditions, illegal charges or unnecessary battles to reclaim deposits. While the Renters' Reform Bill represents progress, its success depends on robust enforcement of existing and new rules."

With private rents rising at their fastest pace in decades, tenants facing these challenges might consider documenting all communications with agents and familiarising themselves with their rights under current legislation. Those encountering unlawful practices could contact organisations like Citizens Advice or their local council's housing team for support.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Nearly half of UK private renters have encountered letting agents breaking industry rules over the past three years, according to new research from Citizens Advice. The findings reveal widespread issues ranging from delayed emergency repairs to unlawful fees, leaving millions of tenants facing substandard service amidst record rental prices.

Widespread problems in private rentals

The charity's study suggests approximately four million renters may have experienced practices that contravene regulations. Among the most concerning findings, 68% of tenants reporting emergencies like gas leaks or electrical faults waited over 24 hours for action, with 29% stating urgent repairs were never completed. The research also uncovered instances where agents improperly pushed zero-deposit schemes or charged prohibited inventory fees.

Deposit return times emerged as another significant issue, with more than half of tenants waiting beyond the legal 10-day period to recover some or all of their deposit. These problems persist despite existing protections under the Tenant Fees Act 2019, which banned most letting fees in England and introduced deposit caps.

Renters' Reform Bill under scrutiny

Citizens Advice warns these systemic failures could undermine the government's forthcoming Renters' Reform Bill unless accompanied by stronger enforcement. The charity argues that without proper regulation and accountability, the legislation may fail to deliver meaningful improvements for tenants already struggling with affordability pressures.

Tom MacInnes, Citizens Advice's Director of Policy, commented: "Private renters are paying record amounts yet receiving substandard service. No tenant should face dangerous living conditions, illegal charges or unnecessary battles to reclaim deposits. While the Renters' Reform Bill represents progress, its success depends on robust enforcement of existing and new rules."

With private rents rising at their fastest pace in decades, tenants facing these challenges might consider documenting all communications with agents and familiarising themselves with their rights under current legislation. Those encountering unlawful practices could contact organisations like Citizens Advice or their local council's housing team for support.

item.title: Tax raid on landlords? Think tank urges Labour to hit rental income
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The New Economics Foundation (NEF), a leading think tank, has proposed that rental income should be subject to National Insurance contributions (NICs). This suggestion, aimed at addressing perceived tax advantages for landlords, could potentially generate billions for the Treasury if implemented. The NEF argues that rental income is currently exempt from NICs, unlike earnings from employment, creating what it describes as an "unwarranted tax exemption."

The proposal comes amidst growing scrutiny of the private rented sector and its tax treatment. Whilst landlords already pay income tax on rental profits, rental income is categorised as investment income, meaning NICs do not apply. However, landlords can opt to make voluntary contributions to safeguard their state pension entitlements. The NEF believes aligning rental income with employment earnings could raise up to £3.2bn annually.

Potential Implications for Landlords

If the government adopts the NEF's recommendation, landlords could face higher operating costs, which might lead to increased rents for tenants. Critics argue that adding NICs to the existing tax burden could further squeeze landlords' margins, potentially discouraging investment in the private rented sector. Chris Norris, chief policy officer at the National Residential Landlords Association, described the proposal as "disastrous" for landlords, warning that costs would likely be passed on to tenants.

The NEF has suggested that any extension of NICs to rental income could be accompanied by the reintroduction of mortgage interest relief. This would allow landlords to offset finance costs, ensuring tax is applied to profits rather than gross income. Such a move could mitigate some of the financial pressures on landlords whilst addressing the perceived imbalance in tax treatment.

Political and Economic Context

The proposal arrives ahead of the Autumn Budget, where Chancellor Rachel Reeves is expected to outline the government’s tax and spending plans. With the housing market and rental sector under significant strain, any changes to tax policy could have wide-ranging implications. The NEF’s recommendation highlights ongoing debates about fairness in the tax system and the role of private landlords in the housing market.

Whilst the suggestion has sparked controversy, it underscores the need for a balanced approach to tax reform. Policymakers may wish to consider the potential impact on both landlords and tenants, ensuring any changes support a sustainable and fair rental market.

As discussions continue, landlords and tenants alike will be watching closely for updates on how this proposal, and broader tax reforms, could shape the future of the UK’s housing landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The New Economics Foundation (NEF), a leading think tank, has proposed that rental income should be subject to National Insurance contributions (NICs). This suggestion, aimed at addressing perceived tax advantages for landlords, could potentially generate billions for the Treasury if implemented. The NEF argues that rental income is currently exempt from NICs, unlike earnings from employment, creating what it describes as an "unwarranted tax exemption."

The proposal comes amidst growing scrutiny of the private rented sector and its tax treatment. Whilst landlords already pay income tax on rental profits, rental income is categorised as investment income, meaning NICs do not apply. However, landlords can opt to make voluntary contributions to safeguard their state pension entitlements. The NEF believes aligning rental income with employment earnings could raise up to £3.2bn annually.

Potential Implications for Landlords

If the government adopts the NEF's recommendation, landlords could face higher operating costs, which might lead to increased rents for tenants. Critics argue that adding NICs to the existing tax burden could further squeeze landlords' margins, potentially discouraging investment in the private rented sector. Chris Norris, chief policy officer at the National Residential Landlords Association, described the proposal as "disastrous" for landlords, warning that costs would likely be passed on to tenants.

The NEF has suggested that any extension of NICs to rental income could be accompanied by the reintroduction of mortgage interest relief. This would allow landlords to offset finance costs, ensuring tax is applied to profits rather than gross income. Such a move could mitigate some of the financial pressures on landlords whilst addressing the perceived imbalance in tax treatment.

Political and Economic Context

The proposal arrives ahead of the Autumn Budget, where Chancellor Rachel Reeves is expected to outline the government’s tax and spending plans. With the housing market and rental sector under significant strain, any changes to tax policy could have wide-ranging implications. The NEF’s recommendation highlights ongoing debates about fairness in the tax system and the role of private landlords in the housing market.

Whilst the suggestion has sparked controversy, it underscores the need for a balanced approach to tax reform. Policymakers may wish to consider the potential impact on both landlords and tenants, ensuring any changes support a sustainable and fair rental market.

As discussions continue, landlords and tenants alike will be watching closely for updates on how this proposal, and broader tax reforms, could shape the future of the UK’s housing landscape.

item.title: Hammered! Property auctions spark complaint surge
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Property auctions, whilst accounting for a small percentage of overall home sales, have become a significant source of complaints in the UK housing market. According to a recent report by The Property Ombudsman (TPO), auction sales make up just 2% of residential transactions but are responsible for 5% of all complaints received in 2025. Alarmingly, they account for 9% of grievances specifically related to residential sales. With over 300 auction-related complaints resolved last year, it’s clear that buyers and prospective buyers are often left unsatisfied with their experiences.

Understanding the Risks of Property Auctions

One of the key findings of the report is that many consumers enter the auction process without fully understanding the risks, costs, and obligations involved. The information provided to buyers can be complex and difficult to navigate, leading to misunderstandings and disputes. A particular area of confusion is the Modern Method of Auction, which, whilst appearing similar to traditional estate agency sales, involves different timelines, fees, and contractual commitments. As this method has grown in popularity, so too have the complaints associated with it.

Reservation fees, a common feature of auction sales, have emerged as a major source of contention. Some buyers mistakenly believe these fees are refundable if a sale falls through or that they will be deducted from the purchase price. In many cases, these misunderstandings stem from unclear or insufficiently prominent terms and conditions. The report emphasises the importance of clear communication, particularly in a process where buyers are often required to make quick decisions and face financial consequences earlier than in conventional sales.

Common Complaints and Missteps

The report highlights several recurring issues in auction-related complaints. Inaccurate property descriptions, incomplete legal packs, unexpected fees, and a lack of clarity around the auction process were among the most frequent grievances. Buyers, who accounted for 68% of residential auction complaints, often felt they were not provided with sufficient information to make informed decisions. This lack of transparency can lead to costly mistakes and dissatisfaction.

Lesley Horton, Chief Property Ombudsman, commented: “Property auctions can offer speed, certainty, and a defined route to sale. However, they also involve a different level of commitment, pace, and risk. Our casework shows that complaints can arise where consumers do not clearly understand the process, where key information is not provided at the right time, or where businesses rely on information being available rather than ensuring it is understood.”

Advice for Prospective Auction Buyers

For those considering purchasing a property at auction, it could be worth taking extra steps to ensure they are fully prepared. Conducting thorough research, seeking professional advice, and carefully reviewing all documentation, including terms and conditions, may help mitigate risks. Buyers might also wish to inspect the property in person and request clarification on any unclear aspects of the process before proceeding.

Whilst property auctions can be an effective way to secure a home quickly, understanding the unique challenges and responsibilities involved is crucial. By being well-informed and vigilant, prospective buyers can reduce the likelihood of encountering issues and make the most of the opportunities auctions can offer.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Property auctions, whilst accounting for a small percentage of overall home sales, have become a significant source of complaints in the UK housing market. According to a recent report by The Property Ombudsman (TPO), auction sales make up just 2% of residential transactions but are responsible for 5% of all complaints received in 2025. Alarmingly, they account for 9% of grievances specifically related to residential sales. With over 300 auction-related complaints resolved last year, it’s clear that buyers and prospective buyers are often left unsatisfied with their experiences.

Understanding the Risks of Property Auctions

One of the key findings of the report is that many consumers enter the auction process without fully understanding the risks, costs, and obligations involved. The information provided to buyers can be complex and difficult to navigate, leading to misunderstandings and disputes. A particular area of confusion is the Modern Method of Auction, which, whilst appearing similar to traditional estate agency sales, involves different timelines, fees, and contractual commitments. As this method has grown in popularity, so too have the complaints associated with it.

Reservation fees, a common feature of auction sales, have emerged as a major source of contention. Some buyers mistakenly believe these fees are refundable if a sale falls through or that they will be deducted from the purchase price. In many cases, these misunderstandings stem from unclear or insufficiently prominent terms and conditions. The report emphasises the importance of clear communication, particularly in a process where buyers are often required to make quick decisions and face financial consequences earlier than in conventional sales.

Common Complaints and Missteps

The report highlights several recurring issues in auction-related complaints. Inaccurate property descriptions, incomplete legal packs, unexpected fees, and a lack of clarity around the auction process were among the most frequent grievances. Buyers, who accounted for 68% of residential auction complaints, often felt they were not provided with sufficient information to make informed decisions. This lack of transparency can lead to costly mistakes and dissatisfaction.

Lesley Horton, Chief Property Ombudsman, commented: “Property auctions can offer speed, certainty, and a defined route to sale. However, they also involve a different level of commitment, pace, and risk. Our casework shows that complaints can arise where consumers do not clearly understand the process, where key information is not provided at the right time, or where businesses rely on information being available rather than ensuring it is understood.”

Advice for Prospective Auction Buyers

For those considering purchasing a property at auction, it could be worth taking extra steps to ensure they are fully prepared. Conducting thorough research, seeking professional advice, and carefully reviewing all documentation, including terms and conditions, may help mitigate risks. Buyers might also wish to inspect the property in person and request clarification on any unclear aspects of the process before proceeding.

Whilst property auctions can be an effective way to secure a home quickly, understanding the unique challenges and responsibilities involved is crucial. By being well-informed and vigilant, prospective buyers can reduce the likelihood of encountering issues and make the most of the opportunities auctions can offer.

item.title: Industry trial aims to give homeowners easier access to property data
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A new industry trial could transform how homeowners access vital property information, with HM Land Registry data potentially becoming available through digital logbook platforms. The proof-of-concept project, developed in partnership between the Residential Logbook Association (RLBA) and HM Land Registry (HMLR), aims to give property owners direct access to their title register details without needing to purchase documents or rely on professionals.

Simplifying property information access

Currently, homeowners typically need to pay for official copies of title documents or depend on estate agents and conveyancers to obtain Land Registry details. The trial explores whether integrating this data into digital property logbooks could help individuals better understand their property records and prepare more effectively for future sales. Participating logbook providers will experiment with different ways to present and explain the information, while researchers monitor how consumers interact with it.

Sally Holdway, buying and selling lead at RLBA, noted: "Early access to property data could help identify and correct errors before they cause transaction delays. Many sales fall through due to outdated Land Registry records that haven't been updated since the last property transfer." The initiative may particularly benefit those planning to sell, allowing them to verify details and address discrepancies well in advance.

Supporting smoother property transactions

The trial forms part of HMLR's Strategy 2025+, which focuses on improving customer experience in property transactions. Terry Robertson, HMLR's deputy director of strategy, explained: "Reliable information at the right time makes a significant difference in what's often people's most important financial transaction. This project helps us understand how homeowners might securely access and confidently use their property data earlier in the process."

Participants will include both prospective sellers and recent buyers, with some using existing logbooks and others creating new ones as part of the trial. Whilst the data won't be transaction-ready (official copies will still need purchasing for legal purposes), homeowners may use it to create digital sales packs within their logbooks. This could potentially streamline the sales process by ensuring all parties have access to accurate information from the outset.

As the property market continues its digital transformation, this trial represents another step towards making essential information more accessible to homeowners. The findings may shape how property data is shared and used in future transactions, potentially reducing delays and failed sales caused by information gaps.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A new industry trial could transform how homeowners access vital property information, with HM Land Registry data potentially becoming available through digital logbook platforms. The proof-of-concept project, developed in partnership between the Residential Logbook Association (RLBA) and HM Land Registry (HMLR), aims to give property owners direct access to their title register details without needing to purchase documents or rely on professionals.

Simplifying property information access

Currently, homeowners typically need to pay for official copies of title documents or depend on estate agents and conveyancers to obtain Land Registry details. The trial explores whether integrating this data into digital property logbooks could help individuals better understand their property records and prepare more effectively for future sales. Participating logbook providers will experiment with different ways to present and explain the information, while researchers monitor how consumers interact with it.

Sally Holdway, buying and selling lead at RLBA, noted: "Early access to property data could help identify and correct errors before they cause transaction delays. Many sales fall through due to outdated Land Registry records that haven't been updated since the last property transfer." The initiative may particularly benefit those planning to sell, allowing them to verify details and address discrepancies well in advance.

Supporting smoother property transactions

The trial forms part of HMLR's Strategy 2025+, which focuses on improving customer experience in property transactions. Terry Robertson, HMLR's deputy director of strategy, explained: "Reliable information at the right time makes a significant difference in what's often people's most important financial transaction. This project helps us understand how homeowners might securely access and confidently use their property data earlier in the process."

Participants will include both prospective sellers and recent buyers, with some using existing logbooks and others creating new ones as part of the trial. Whilst the data won't be transaction-ready (official copies will still need purchasing for legal purposes), homeowners may use it to create digital sales packs within their logbooks. This could potentially streamline the sales process by ensuring all parties have access to accurate information from the outset.

As the property market continues its digital transformation, this trial represents another step towards making essential information more accessible to homeowners. The findings may shape how property data is shared and used in future transactions, potentially reducing delays and failed sales caused by information gaps.

item.title: Mortgage approvals rise in April but…
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK mortgage approvals reached a 15-month high in April, according to the latest Bank of England figures, suggesting resilience in the housing market despite economic headwinds. Lenders approved 65,945 home purchase loans during the month – a 3.1% increase from March and significantly higher than economists' predictions of 62,000 approvals. This marks the strongest performance since January 2025, though the market continues to face challenges from rising borrowing costs and global uncertainty.

Mixed signals for housing market

While mortgage approvals showed strength, other indicators paint a more nuanced picture. Net mortgage borrowing fell sharply to £4.4bn – the lowest level since October 2025 – reflecting completed transactions that typically lag behind approval figures. The effective interest rate on new mortgages also edged up to 4.08%, adding pressure to household budgets. Property experts note these conflicting trends suggest buyers remain active but cautious, with many potentially rushing to secure deals before further rate rises.

Market analysts highlight that April's approvals likely reflect decisions made before recent geopolitical tensions escalated. Jason Tebb of OnTheMarket observed: "Our sentiment index shows buyers and sellers adapting to conditions, with attitudes towards affordability and property values remaining surprisingly stable despite economic turbulence." The figures also follow the Bank of England's decision to hold base rates steady, which may have provided some reassurance to prospective homeowners.

Challenges ahead for borrowers

Looking forward, the market faces several potential obstacles. Rising energy prices and persistent inflation continue to squeeze household finances, while global conflicts create uncertainty in financial markets. Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets noted that while April's performance represented a "welcome rebound" from a sluggish start to the year, the recovery remains fragile. The approval figures, though strong, still sit just 2% above the ten-year average, suggesting growth remains modest by historical standards.

For those considering entering the market, it could be worth monitoring how these trends develop in coming months. Mortgage rates have shown volatility recently, and homeowners may wish to explore their options carefully as the economic landscape evolves. With approvals indicating sustained demand but borrowing costs rising, the summer months may prove crucial for understanding the market's direction.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK mortgage approvals reached a 15-month high in April, according to the latest Bank of England figures, suggesting resilience in the housing market despite economic headwinds. Lenders approved 65,945 home purchase loans during the month – a 3.1% increase from March and significantly higher than economists' predictions of 62,000 approvals. This marks the strongest performance since January 2025, though the market continues to face challenges from rising borrowing costs and global uncertainty.

Mixed signals for housing market

While mortgage approvals showed strength, other indicators paint a more nuanced picture. Net mortgage borrowing fell sharply to £4.4bn – the lowest level since October 2025 – reflecting completed transactions that typically lag behind approval figures. The effective interest rate on new mortgages also edged up to 4.08%, adding pressure to household budgets. Property experts note these conflicting trends suggest buyers remain active but cautious, with many potentially rushing to secure deals before further rate rises.

Market analysts highlight that April's approvals likely reflect decisions made before recent geopolitical tensions escalated. Jason Tebb of OnTheMarket observed: "Our sentiment index shows buyers and sellers adapting to conditions, with attitudes towards affordability and property values remaining surprisingly stable despite economic turbulence." The figures also follow the Bank of England's decision to hold base rates steady, which may have provided some reassurance to prospective homeowners.

Challenges ahead for borrowers

Looking forward, the market faces several potential obstacles. Rising energy prices and persistent inflation continue to squeeze household finances, while global conflicts create uncertainty in financial markets. Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets noted that while April's performance represented a "welcome rebound" from a sluggish start to the year, the recovery remains fragile. The approval figures, though strong, still sit just 2% above the ten-year average, suggesting growth remains modest by historical standards.

For those considering entering the market, it could be worth monitoring how these trends develop in coming months. Mortgage rates have shown volatility recently, and homeowners may wish to explore their options carefully as the economic landscape evolves. With approvals indicating sustained demand but borrowing costs rising, the summer months may prove crucial for understanding the market's direction.

item.title: Rents begin to bounce back after April price drop
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK Rental Market Shows Signs of Cooling Despite Supply Pressures

Recent data indicates that UK rental price growth continues to moderate, with annual inflation reaching its lowest point in nearly a year. According to the latest rental index figures, average rents across England rose by just 1.7% in May compared to the same period last year, marking the second consecutive month of subdued growth. The average monthly rent now stands at £1,211, a marginal increase from £1,191 in May 2023.

Regional Variations in Rental Performance

The rental market is displaying notable regional disparities. London maintains its position as the strongest performer, with rents climbing 5.6% annually to reach £2,194 per month. This growth occurs against a backdrop of constrained housing supply in the capital, where new property development continues to lag behind targets.

Meanwhile, the North East experienced a significant rebound, with rents jumping 5.5% in May following a 4.9% decline in April. Yorkshire and the Humber also showed resilience with a 3.2% monthly increase. However, several regions recorded month-on-month decreases, including:

  • The East Midlands
  • East of England
  • South West
  • North West

Changing Market Dynamics

The current rental landscape presents some unusual seasonal patterns. For the first time since the pandemic, May rents have failed to surpass their March levels, with average prices remaining slightly below the £1,212 peak recorded earlier in the year. This cooling trend persists despite ongoing supply constraints across much of the country.

Market analysts suggest several factors may be contributing to the moderation in rental growth:

  • Changing migration patterns potentially easing demand
  • Improved affordability pressures as wage growth outpaces rent increases
  • Tenants becoming more resistant to significant price hikes

While the rental market remains competitive in many areas, particularly London, the latest figures suggest a potential shift in dynamics. Prospective tenants might find slightly more favourable conditions in some regions, though supply shortages continue to underpin prices across most of the country.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK Rental Market Shows Signs of Cooling Despite Supply Pressures

Recent data indicates that UK rental price growth continues to moderate, with annual inflation reaching its lowest point in nearly a year. According to the latest rental index figures, average rents across England rose by just 1.7% in May compared to the same period last year, marking the second consecutive month of subdued growth. The average monthly rent now stands at £1,211, a marginal increase from £1,191 in May 2023.

Regional Variations in Rental Performance

The rental market is displaying notable regional disparities. London maintains its position as the strongest performer, with rents climbing 5.6% annually to reach £2,194 per month. This growth occurs against a backdrop of constrained housing supply in the capital, where new property development continues to lag behind targets.

Meanwhile, the North East experienced a significant rebound, with rents jumping 5.5% in May following a 4.9% decline in April. Yorkshire and the Humber also showed resilience with a 3.2% monthly increase. However, several regions recorded month-on-month decreases, including:

  • The East Midlands
  • East of England
  • South West
  • North West

Changing Market Dynamics

The current rental landscape presents some unusual seasonal patterns. For the first time since the pandemic, May rents have failed to surpass their March levels, with average prices remaining slightly below the £1,212 peak recorded earlier in the year. This cooling trend persists despite ongoing supply constraints across much of the country.

Market analysts suggest several factors may be contributing to the moderation in rental growth:

  • Changing migration patterns potentially easing demand
  • Improved affordability pressures as wage growth outpaces rent increases
  • Tenants becoming more resistant to significant price hikes

While the rental market remains competitive in many areas, particularly London, the latest figures suggest a potential shift in dynamics. Prospective tenants might find slightly more favourable conditions in some regions, though supply shortages continue to underpin prices across most of the country.

item.title: UK property transactions down 3% in April
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

UK property transactions saw a modest 3% decline in April compared to March, according to the latest figures. Whilst this represents a slight monthly dip, analysts note the housing market continues to demonstrate notable resilience given current economic headwinds. The relatively stable transaction levels suggest sustained demand from both homeowners and investors.

Market shows stability despite economic pressures

The small reduction in property transactions comes against a backdrop of rising interest rates and persistent inflation. Industry observers highlight that the marginal decrease indicates the market is holding up better than some forecasts predicted. Several factors may be contributing to this stability:

  • Strong employment figures supporting buyer confidence
  • Limited housing stock maintaining price stability
  • Lenders offering competitive mortgage products

Regional variations in transaction levels

Whilst national figures show a modest decline, regional performance varies significantly. Some areas of the UK continue to see robust activity, particularly in more affordable markets where first-time buyers remain active. The market appears to be adjusting to higher borrowing costs rather than experiencing a significant downturn.

Those considering entering the property market might wish to research local trends carefully, as conditions can differ markedly between postcodes. Mortgage brokers often highlight the importance of understanding regional dynamics when making property decisions.

With the Bank of England's base rate decisions continuing to influence mortgage pricing, many potential buyers are weighing their options carefully. The coming months will reveal whether April's slight dip represents a temporary fluctuation or the beginning of a more sustained trend.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

UK property transactions saw a modest 3% decline in April compared to March, according to the latest figures. Whilst this represents a slight monthly dip, analysts note the housing market continues to demonstrate notable resilience given current economic headwinds. The relatively stable transaction levels suggest sustained demand from both homeowners and investors.

Market shows stability despite economic pressures

The small reduction in property transactions comes against a backdrop of rising interest rates and persistent inflation. Industry observers highlight that the marginal decrease indicates the market is holding up better than some forecasts predicted. Several factors may be contributing to this stability:

  • Strong employment figures supporting buyer confidence
  • Limited housing stock maintaining price stability
  • Lenders offering competitive mortgage products

Regional variations in transaction levels

Whilst national figures show a modest decline, regional performance varies significantly. Some areas of the UK continue to see robust activity, particularly in more affordable markets where first-time buyers remain active. The market appears to be adjusting to higher borrowing costs rather than experiencing a significant downturn.

Those considering entering the property market might wish to research local trends carefully, as conditions can differ markedly between postcodes. Mortgage brokers often highlight the importance of understanding regional dynamics when making property decisions.

With the Bank of England's base rate decisions continuing to influence mortgage pricing, many potential buyers are weighing their options carefully. The coming months will reveal whether April's slight dip represents a temporary fluctuation or the beginning of a more sustained trend.

item.title: Rental yields stabilise to 6.5% in Q1
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental Yields Hold Steady at 6.5% as Market Shows Resilience

Private rental sector investors may find reassurance in new figures showing stabilised yields during the first quarter of the year. Average rental yields across the UK have settled at 6.5%, suggesting continued profitability for landlords despite broader economic pressures.

Occupancy Rates Underpin Stable Returns

The consistent performance appears linked to sustained tenant demand, with occupancy levels remaining robust across most regions. Property investors might note that this stability comes amidst:

  • Gradual easing of inflation pressures
  • Relatively strong employment figures
  • Ongoing housing supply constraints

Market analysts suggest these factors have collectively helped maintain equilibrium between rental prices and property values, preventing the yield compression some had predicted.

Regional Variations Persist

Whilst the national average provides a helpful benchmark, experienced investors will be aware that significant regional disparities continue. Northern cities have reportedly maintained stronger yields compared to southern counterparts, though London's prime postcodes continue to command premium rents.

Those reviewing their property portfolios might consider how local market conditions could affect their specific investments, particularly regarding:

  • Local employment trends
  • New housing developments
  • Transport infrastructure projects

With the Bank of England's base rate decisions continuing to influence mortgage costs, the stability in rental yields could prove significant for landlords assessing their financing options. As always, professional financial advice remains essential when making investment decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental Yields Hold Steady at 6.5% as Market Shows Resilience

Private rental sector investors may find reassurance in new figures showing stabilised yields during the first quarter of the year. Average rental yields across the UK have settled at 6.5%, suggesting continued profitability for landlords despite broader economic pressures.

Whilst the national average provides a helpful benchmark, experienced investors will be aware that significant regional disparities continue. Northern cities have reportedly maintained stronger yields compared to southern counterparts, though London's prime postcodes continue to command premium rents.

Those reviewing their property portfolios might consider how local market conditions could affect their specific investments, particularly regarding:

  • Local employment trends
  • New housing developments
  • Transport infrastructure projects

With the Bank of England's base rate decisions continuing to influence mortgage costs, the stability in rental yields could prove significant for landlords assessing their financing options. As always, professional financial advice remains essential when making investment decisions.

item.title: First Home Fund boost for Scottish housing market
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Scottish housing market has received a potential boost with the relaunch of the First Home Fund, an initiative designed to help first-time buyers secure their initial property purchase. The government-backed scheme offers interest-free loans towards deposit costs, potentially making homeownership more accessible for those struggling to save the traditional upfront amounts.

How the First Home Fund works

The revamped First Home Fund provides eligible buyers in Scotland with up to £25,000 (or 49% of the property value) as an interest-free loan towards their deposit. Unlike some shared equity schemes, participants own 100% of their property from the outset. The loan becomes repayable when the home is sold, with the amount adjusted according to the property's current market value.

Key features of the scheme include:

  • Available to first-time buyers and previous homeowners who haven't owned property in the last three years
  • Can be combined with other Scottish government schemes like LIFT (Low-cost Initiative for First Time Buyers)
  • Minimum personal deposit contribution of £1,000 required
  • Property price cap of £175,000 for most areas

Potential impact on the Scottish property market

Industry observers suggest the scheme's return could stimulate activity in Scotland's first-time buyer market, particularly in areas where property prices remain relatively affordable. The interest-free nature of the loan may prove particularly attractive compared to traditional mortgage products or alternative deposit assistance schemes.

Prospective buyers might wish to consider that:

  • The scheme operates on a first-come, first-served basis with limited funding available
  • Approved lenders may have specific mortgage criteria alongside the scheme requirements
  • Future property value changes will affect the final repayment amount

With housing affordability remaining a challenge across the UK, the First Home Fund represents one of several options available to Scottish buyers exploring routes onto the property ladder. Those considering the scheme may benefit from consulting an independent mortgage adviser to understand how it could fit with their individual circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Scottish housing market has received a potential boost with the relaunch of the First Home Fund, an initiative designed to help first-time buyers secure their initial property purchase. The government-backed scheme offers interest-free loans towards deposit costs, potentially making homeownership more accessible for those struggling to save the traditional upfront amounts.

How the First Home Fund works

The revamped First Home Fund provides eligible buyers in Scotland with up to £25,000 (or 49% of the property value) as an interest-free loan towards their deposit. Unlike some shared equity schemes, participants own 100% of their property from the outset. The loan becomes repayable when the home is sold, with the amount adjusted according to the property's current market value.

Key features of the scheme include:

  • Available to first-time buyers and previous homeowners who haven't owned property in the last three years
  • Can be combined with other Scottish government schemes like LIFT (Low-cost Initiative for First Time Buyers)
  • Minimum personal deposit contribution of £1,000 required
  • Property price cap of £175,000 for most areas

Potential impact on the Scottish property market

Industry observers suggest the scheme's return could stimulate activity in Scotland's first-time buyer market, particularly in areas where property prices remain relatively affordable. The interest-free nature of the loan may prove particularly attractive compared to traditional mortgage products or alternative deposit assistance schemes.

Prospective buyers might wish to consider that:

  • The scheme operates on a first-come, first-served basis with limited funding available
  • Approved lenders may have specific mortgage criteria alongside the scheme requirements
  • Future property value changes will affect the final repayment amount

With housing affordability remaining a challenge across the UK, the First Home Fund represents one of several options available to Scottish buyers exploring routes onto the property ladder. Those considering the scheme may benefit from consulting an independent mortgage adviser to understand how it could fit with their individual circumstances.

item.title: Seaside investment hotspots revealed
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research highlights several coastal towns across the UK where property prices are surging, offering potential opportunities for investors and homeowners alike. The analysis reveals Bootle in Merseyside leading the pack with an 11% annual increase in average asking prices, followed closely by Crosby in Liverpool and Penarth in South Glamorgan, both recording 9% growth.

Coastal towns with strongest price growth

The study identifies several seaside locations experiencing notable property value increases:

  • Bootle, Merseyside: 11% annual price growth
  • Crosby, Liverpool: 9% increase
  • Penarth, South Glamorgan: 9% rise
  • Llantwit Major, South Glamorgan: 8% uplift
  • Llanelli, Carmarthenshire: 7% growth

These figures suggest a growing appetite for coastal properties, potentially driven by changing lifestyle preferences following the pandemic and the increasing popularity of hybrid working arrangements. The data indicates that buyers are looking beyond traditional city centres for more affordable options with quality-of-life benefits.

Factors driving seaside property demand

Several elements may be contributing to this coastal property boom. Many of these locations offer relatively affordable housing compared to nearby cities, coupled with the appeal of seaside living. Improved transport links and digital infrastructure have also made these areas more accessible for those who split their time between home and office working.

Prospective buyers might consider researching local amenities, transport connections and future development plans when evaluating these markets. It could be worth consulting with local estate agents to understand the specific dynamics of each area, as coastal markets can vary significantly even within the same region.

While these figures show promising growth, it's worth remembering that past performance doesn't guarantee future results. Property values can fluctuate based on numerous economic factors, and thorough research remains essential before making any investment decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research highlights several coastal towns across the UK where property prices are surging, offering potential opportunities for investors and homeowners alike. The analysis reveals Bootle in Merseyside leading the pack with an 11% annual increase in average asking prices, followed closely by Crosby in Liverpool and Penarth in South Glamorgan, both recording 9% growth.

Coastal towns with strongest price growth

The study identifies several seaside locations experiencing notable property value increases:

  • Bootle, Merseyside: 11% annual price growth
  • Crosby, Liverpool: 9% increase
  • Penarth, South Glamorgan: 9% rise
  • Llantwit Major, South Glamorgan: 8% uplift
  • Llanelli, Carmarthenshire: 7% growth

These figures suggest a growing appetite for coastal properties, potentially driven by changing lifestyle preferences following the pandemic and the increasing popularity of hybrid working arrangements. The data indicates that buyers are looking beyond traditional city centres for more affordable options with quality-of-life benefits.

Factors driving seaside property demand

Several elements may be contributing to this coastal property boom. Many of these locations offer relatively affordable housing compared to nearby cities, coupled with the appeal of seaside living. Improved transport links and digital infrastructure have also made these areas more accessible for those who split their time between home and office working.

Prospective buyers might consider researching local amenities, transport connections and future development plans when evaluating these markets. It could be worth consulting with local estate agents to understand the specific dynamics of each area, as coastal markets can vary significantly even within the same region.

While these figures show promising growth, it's worth remembering that past performance doesn't guarantee future results. Property values can fluctuate based on numerous economic factors, and thorough research remains essential before making any investment decisions.

item.title: The benefits of made to measure cupboard doors for B2B projects
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Commercial property owners and developers are increasingly turning to made-to-measure cupboard doors as a way to enhance both functionality and aesthetic appeal in business premises. These bespoke solutions offer distinct advantages over standard off-the-shelf options, particularly when it comes to maximising space efficiency and creating tailored interiors that meet specific operational needs.

Why bespoke storage solutions matter for commercial properties

In commercial settings, every square foot counts. Made-to-measure cupboard doors allow businesses to:

  • Utilise awkward or irregular spaces that standard units wouldn't fit
  • Create seamless storage solutions that complement the overall interior design
  • Improve workflow efficiency with storage tailored to specific business requirements
  • Enhance durability with materials selected for high-traffic environments

For landlords and property investors, these custom solutions can potentially add value to commercial units whilst addressing the practical needs of tenants across various sectors, from hospitality to professional services.

Long-term benefits for property investors

Investing in quality fitted storage solutions could prove advantageous for those managing commercial property portfolios. Well-designed made-to-measure cupboards often:

  • Require less maintenance than standard units due to superior materials and construction
  • Extend the lifespan of storage areas, reducing replacement costs
  • Create a more premium feel that may justify higher rental values
  • Provide flexibility to accommodate different tenant requirements over time

Whilst the initial outlay for bespoke joinery might be higher than mass-produced alternatives, the potential long-term savings and value-add could make it a worthwhile consideration for commercial property owners.

As businesses continue to prioritise efficient use of space and professional presentation, made-to-measure storage solutions are likely to remain an important feature in commercial interior projects. Property investors might consider how these tailored elements could enhance both the functionality and marketability of their assets.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Commercial property owners and developers are increasingly turning to made-to-measure cupboard doors as a way to enhance both functionality and aesthetic appeal in business premises. These bespoke solutions offer distinct advantages over standard off-the-shelf options, particularly when it comes to maximising space efficiency and creating tailored interiors that meet specific operational needs.

Why bespoke storage solutions matter for commercial properties

In commercial settings, every square foot counts. Made-to-measure cupboard doors allow businesses to:

  • Utilise awkward or irregular spaces that standard units wouldn't fit
  • Create seamless storage solutions that complement the overall interior design
  • Improve workflow efficiency with storage tailored to specific business requirements
  • Enhance durability with materials selected for high-traffic environments

For landlords and property investors, these custom solutions can potentially add value to commercial units whilst addressing the practical needs of tenants across various sectors, from hospitality to professional services.

Long-term benefits for property investors

Investing in quality fitted storage solutions could prove advantageous for those managing commercial property portfolios. Well-designed made-to-measure cupboards often:

  • Require less maintenance than standard units due to superior materials and construction
  • Extend the lifespan of storage areas, reducing replacement costs
  • Create a more premium feel that may justify higher rental values
  • Provide flexibility to accommodate different tenant requirements over time

Whilst the initial outlay for bespoke joinery might be higher than mass-produced alternatives, the potential long-term savings and value-add could make it a worthwhile consideration for commercial property owners.

As businesses continue to prioritise efficient use of space and professional presentation, made-to-measure storage solutions are likely to remain an important feature in commercial interior projects. Property investors might consider how these tailored elements could enhance both the functionality and marketability of their assets.

item.title: The Hidden Costs of Buying the Wrong Investment Property: 7 Mistakes Investors Make Before They Buy
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Whilst many property investors focus heavily on rental yields and market trends, some of the most expensive mistakes occur during the initial property search and purchase stages. Overlooking key factors early in the process could lead to costly consequences that only become apparent months or years later. Being aware of common pitfalls might help investors make more informed decisions when building their portfolios.

Common Pre-Purchase Oversights

Investors often concentrate on the financial aspects of property investment whilst underestimating other crucial considerations. Some frequent oversights include:

  • Failing to properly research local rental demand and tenant profiles
  • Underestimating ongoing maintenance costs for older properties
  • Overlooking restrictive covenants or unusual lease terms
  • Not accounting for potential void periods in cash flow calculations
  • Ignoring upcoming local developments that could affect property values

These factors might not immediately impact profitability but could significantly affect long-term returns. Investors might wish to create a comprehensive checklist that goes beyond basic financial metrics when evaluating potential purchases.

Hidden Costs That Can Erode Returns

Some expenses only become apparent after completing a purchase. Unexpected costs that could affect an investment's performance include:

  • Higher-than-anticipated renovation costs to make properties lettable
  • Unexpected service charges or ground rent increases in leasehold properties
  • Compliance costs for new energy efficiency regulations
  • Legal fees for dealing with problematic tenancies or disputes
  • Insurance premiums for properties in flood-risk areas

Conducting thorough due diligence, including detailed property surveys and legal checks, might help identify potential issues before exchange. Investors may also consider building larger financial buffers to account for unforeseen expenses.

Property investment requires careful planning beyond simple yield calculations. By paying attention to often-overlooked factors during the purchasing process, investors might avoid costly mistakes and build more resilient portfolios. Professional advice from qualified mortgage brokers and property specialists could provide valuable insights tailored to individual circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Whilst many property investors focus heavily on rental yields and market trends, some of the most expensive mistakes occur during the initial property search and purchase stages. Overlooking key factors early in the process could lead to costly consequences that only become apparent months or years later. Being aware of common pitfalls might help investors make more informed decisions when building their portfolios.

Common Pre-Purchase Oversights

Investors often concentrate on the financial aspects of property investment whilst underestimating other crucial considerations. Some frequent oversights include:

  • Failing to properly research local rental demand and tenant profiles
  • Underestimating ongoing maintenance costs for older properties
  • Overlooking restrictive covenants or unusual lease terms
  • Not accounting for potential void periods in cash flow calculations
  • Ignoring upcoming local developments that could affect property values

These factors might not immediately impact profitability but could significantly affect long-term returns. Investors might wish to create a comprehensive checklist that goes beyond basic financial metrics when evaluating potential purchases.

Hidden Costs That Can Erode Returns

Some expenses only become apparent after completing a purchase. Unexpected costs that could affect an investment's performance include:

  • Higher-than-anticipated renovation costs to make properties lettable
  • Unexpected service charges or ground rent increases in leasehold properties
  • Compliance costs for new energy efficiency regulations
  • Legal fees for dealing with problematic tenancies or disputes
  • Insurance premiums for properties in flood-risk areas

Conducting thorough due diligence, including detailed property surveys and legal checks, might help identify potential issues before exchange. Investors may also consider building larger financial buffers to account for unforeseen expenses.

Property investment requires careful planning beyond simple yield calculations. By paying attention to often-overlooked factors during the purchasing process, investors might avoid costly mistakes and build more resilient portfolios. Professional advice from qualified mortgage brokers and property specialists could provide valuable insights tailored to individual circumstances.

item.title: Is the landlord right to claim for cleaning at the end of the tenancy?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlord awarded cleaning costs after tenant dispute

A recent tenancy dispute has highlighted the importance of understanding cleaning obligations when moving out of a rental property. The case involved a landlord claiming £245 for professional cleaning after their tenant vacated, arguing the property hadn't been returned to its original condition.

The cleaning standards debate

Cleaning disagreements remain one of the most common issues at the end of tenancies. This particular case saw differing expectations between landlord and tenant regarding what constitutes an acceptable standard. The tenant maintained they'd left the property in a "reasonable domestic condition", while the landlord expected cleanliness matching the professional standard documented at check-in.

The inventory report from the tenancy start showed:

  • Professional cleaning throughout
  • Spotless kitchen appliances
  • Limescale-free bathroom fittings
  • Freshly vacuumed carpets

At check-out, evidence showed grease in the oven, limescale deposits, dusty skirting boards and carpet stains. The tenant argued some issues like limescale were due to hard water rather than inadequate cleaning.

Key lessons for tenants and landlords

The adjudicator's decision to award the landlord £180 (reduced from the original claim) provides several important reminders:

  • The check-in inventory sets the cleanliness benchmark tenants need to match
  • "Domestic cleaning" may not meet contractual obligations if the property was professionally cleaned initially
  • Photographic evidence from both parties strengthens any claim
  • Tenants might consider professional cleaning if unsure about meeting required standards

This case illustrates how clear documentation and understanding of tenancy agreements can help prevent disputes. Both landlords and tenants may wish to ensure proper records are kept and expectations are communicated clearly from the outset.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlord awarded cleaning costs after tenant dispute

A recent tenancy dispute has highlighted the importance of understanding cleaning obligations when moving out of a rental property. The case involved a landlord claiming £245 for professional cleaning after their tenant vacated, arguing the property hadn't been returned to its original condition.

The cleaning standards debate

Cleaning disagreements remain one of the most common issues at the end of tenancies. This particular case saw differing expectations between landlord and tenant regarding what constitutes an acceptable standard. The tenant maintained they'd left the property in a "reasonable domestic condition", while the landlord expected cleanliness matching the professional standard documented at check-in.

The inventory report from the tenancy start showed:

  • Professional cleaning throughout
  • Spotless kitchen appliances
  • Limescale-free bathroom fittings
  • Freshly vacuumed carpets

At check-out, evidence showed grease in the oven, limescale deposits, dusty skirting boards and carpet stains. The tenant argued some issues like limescale were due to hard water rather than inadequate cleaning.

Key lessons for tenants and landlords

The adjudicator's decision to award the landlord £180 (reduced from the original claim) provides several important reminders:

  • The check-in inventory sets the cleanliness benchmark tenants need to match
  • "Domestic cleaning" may not meet contractual obligations if the property was professionally cleaned initially
  • Photographic evidence from both parties strengthens any claim
  • Tenants might consider professional cleaning if unsure about meeting required standards

This case illustrates how clear documentation and understanding of tenancy agreements can help prevent disputes. Both landlords and tenants may wish to ensure proper records are kept and expectations are communicated clearly from the outset.

item.title: Former Knight Frank directors launch new property consultancy
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Two seasoned professionals from Knight Frank’s land and development team have embarked on a new venture, establishing a property consultancy tailored to the Midlands market. Will Jordan and Charlie Steele, who boast a combined experience of nearly four decades in the residential development sector, have launched Ashlawn Chase. Based in Henley-in-Arden, the firm aims to provide expert advice to landowners, developers, and investors on land acquisitions, disposals, and strategic opportunities.

Expertise and Focus

Ashlawn Chase positions itself as a consultancy offering straightforward, commercially focused advice, with clients gaining direct access to its founders. Jordan and Steele bring a wealth of experience, having previously advised on a variety of projects across the Midlands, including city centre regeneration schemes, residential developments, and mixed-use sites. Their roles at Knight Frank involved significant transactions and advisory work in Birmingham and the wider region, equipping them with a deep understanding of the local market.

The new consultancy arrives at a time when the residential land market remains active yet challenging. Developers and landowners are increasingly seeking specialist guidance to navigate planning hurdles, viability pressures, and evolving market conditions. Ashlawn Chase aims to address these needs by providing a personal and agile service, grounded in strong relationships and a results-driven approach.

A Personalised Approach

One of the key principles behind Ashlawn Chase is the emphasis on direct access to experienced advisers. Jordan, formerly head of development at Knight Frank’s Birmingham office, highlighted the importance of understanding both opportunities and risks in the current market. “We saw an opportunity to create a business that offers straightforward, commercially focused advice without unnecessary complexity,” he said. “Clients increasingly value direct access to experienced advisers who can guide them through the challenges.”

Steele echoed this sentiment, noting that their shared approach to business has always been relationship-led and commercially grounded. “Launching Ashlawn Chase gives us the opportunity to provide a more personal and agile service to clients across the region,” he explained. This client-centric philosophy is expected to set the consultancy apart in a competitive market.

As Ashlawn Chase begins its journey, its founders are well-positioned to support Midlands-based clients in navigating the complexities of the residential land market. With their extensive experience and commitment to delivering tailored solutions, Jordan and Steele aim to make a significant impact in the region’s property landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Two seasoned professionals from Knight Frank’s land and development team have embarked on a new venture, establishing a property consultancy tailored to the Midlands market. Will Jordan and Charlie Steele, who boast a combined experience of nearly four decades in the residential development sector, have launched Ashlawn Chase. Based in Henley-in-Arden, the firm aims to provide expert advice to landowners, developers, and investors on land acquisitions, disposals, and strategic opportunities.

Expertise and Focus

Ashlawn Chase positions itself as a consultancy offering straightforward, commercially focused advice, with clients gaining direct access to its founders. Jordan and Steele bring a wealth of experience, having previously advised on a variety of projects across the Midlands, including city centre regeneration schemes, residential developments, and mixed-use sites. Their roles at Knight Frank involved significant transactions and advisory work in Birmingham and the wider region, equipping them with a deep understanding of the local market.

The new consultancy arrives at a time when the residential land market remains active yet challenging. Developers and landowners are increasingly seeking specialist guidance to navigate planning hurdles, viability pressures, and evolving market conditions. Ashlawn Chase aims to address these needs by providing a personal and agile service, grounded in strong relationships and a results-driven approach.

A Personalised Approach

One of the key principles behind Ashlawn Chase is the emphasis on direct access to experienced advisers. Jordan, formerly head of development at Knight Frank’s Birmingham office, highlighted the importance of understanding both opportunities and risks in the current market. “We saw an opportunity to create a business that offers straightforward, commercially focused advice without unnecessary complexity,” he said. “Clients increasingly value direct access to experienced advisers who can guide them through the challenges.”

Steele echoed this sentiment, noting that their shared approach to business has always been relationship-led and commercially grounded. “Launching Ashlawn Chase gives us the opportunity to provide a more personal and agile service to clients across the region,” he explained. This client-centric philosophy is expected to set the consultancy apart in a competitive market.

As Ashlawn Chase begins its journey, its founders are well-positioned to support Midlands-based clients in navigating the complexities of the residential land market. With their extensive experience and commitment to delivering tailored solutions, Jordan and Steele aim to make a significant impact in the region’s property landscape.

item.title: Lights, camera, property! Estate agency rolls out the red carpet for film industry
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agency launches filming locations service for UK productions

Carter Jonas has introduced a specialised filming locations service catering to the television, film and photography industries. The new division aims to connect production companies with suitable properties whilst assisting landlords in generating income from their assets. The service will handle everything from initial viewings to post-filming reinstatement.

The estate agency's extensive property portfolio includes residential homes, rural estates and commercial spaces, providing diverse options for location scouts. With the UK's creative industries continuing to thrive, the service arrives as production companies seek fresh and unusual filming settings across the country.

Opportunities for property owners and production teams

The initiative offers potential benefits for both property owners and filmmakers:

  • Landlords with vacant or underutilised buildings may wish to consider this as an alternative revenue stream
  • Production companies gain access to a curated selection of locations through a single point of contact
  • The service manages licensing agreements and on-site coordination, reducing administrative burdens
  • Specialist locations such as industrial sites and rural estates could prove particularly attractive to location managers

Dan Douglas, heading the new division, brings considerable industry experience having previously worked in film and television location services. His team will focus on matching properties to productions while ensuring minimal disruption to owners.

Alexandra Houghton, Carter Jonas' head of commercial, commented: "Our national reach and property expertise position us ideally to support the creative sector whilst helping clients maximise their assets' potential." Chief executive Richard Bruce added that the service reflects the firm's adaptability in responding to evolving market opportunities.

For homeowners with distinctive properties, this development might present an interesting way to generate additional income whilst contributing to the UK's thriving creative economy.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agency launches filming locations service for UK productions

Carter Jonas has introduced a specialised filming locations service catering to the television, film and photography industries. The new division aims to connect production companies with suitable properties whilst assisting landlords in generating income from their assets. The service will handle everything from initial viewings to post-filming reinstatement.

The estate agency's extensive property portfolio includes residential homes, rural estates and commercial spaces, providing diverse options for location scouts. With the UK's creative industries continuing to thrive, the service arrives as production companies seek fresh and unusual filming settings across the country.

Opportunities for property owners and production teams

The initiative offers potential benefits for both property owners and filmmakers:

  • Landlords with vacant or underutilised buildings may wish to consider this as an alternative revenue stream
  • Production companies gain access to a curated selection of locations through a single point of contact
  • The service manages licensing agreements and on-site coordination, reducing administrative burdens
  • Specialist locations such as industrial sites and rural estates could prove particularly attractive to location managers

Dan Douglas, heading the new division, brings considerable industry experience having previously worked in film and television location services. His team will focus on matching properties to productions while ensuring minimal disruption to owners.

Alexandra Houghton, Carter Jonas' head of commercial, commented: "Our national reach and property expertise position us ideally to support the creative sector whilst helping clients maximise their assets' potential." Chief executive Richard Bruce added that the service reflects the firm's adaptability in responding to evolving market opportunities.

For homeowners with distinctive properties, this development might present an interesting way to generate additional income whilst contributing to the UK's thriving creative economy.

item.title: Elite Realty Invest to target double digit growth
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Specialist property investment firm Elite Realty Invest has announced ambitious expansion plans, aiming for double-digit growth by 2026. The company's strategy includes significant operational changes, from workforce expansion to international ventures and securing prime development partnerships.

Expansion plans take shape

Elite Realty Invest has reportedly doubled its workforce in preparation for its growth targets, alongside establishing its first overseas office in Turkey. This international move suggests the firm is looking beyond domestic markets whilst maintaining a strong focus on UK property investment opportunities.

The company has also secured master agent status on several high-end residential developments across the UK. This privileged position gives Elite Realty Invest exclusive access to premium investment properties before they reach the general market, potentially offering clients early opportunities on sought-after developments.

What this means for property investors

For those considering property investment, Elite Realty Invest's expansion could signal several market developments:

  • Increased availability of prime residential properties through exclusive development partnerships
  • Potential for more competitive investment products as firms expand their portfolios
  • Greater international investment options, particularly in emerging markets like Turkey

Property investors might wish to monitor how these expansion plans develop, particularly regarding the types of investment opportunities that may become available through the firm's master agent partnerships. As always, thorough research and independent financial advice remain crucial when considering any property investment.

While Elite Realty Invest's growth ambitions reflect confidence in the property market, market conditions can change rapidly. Investors may find it valuable to keep abreast of both domestic and international property trends when making long-term investment decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Specialist property investment firm Elite Realty Invest has announced ambitious expansion plans, aiming for double-digit growth by 2026. The company's strategy includes significant operational changes, from workforce expansion to international ventures and securing prime development partnerships.

Expansion plans take shape

Elite Realty Invest has reportedly doubled its workforce in preparation for its growth targets, alongside establishing its first overseas office in Turkey. This international move suggests the firm is looking beyond domestic markets whilst maintaining a strong focus on UK property investment opportunities.

The company has also secured master agent status on several high-end residential developments across the UK. This privileged position gives Elite Realty Invest exclusive access to premium investment properties before they reach the general market, potentially offering clients early opportunities on sought-after developments.

What this means for property investors

  • Increased availability of prime residential properties through exclusive development partnerships
  • Potential for more competitive investment products as firms expand their portfolios
  • Greater international investment options, particularly in emerging markets like Turkey

Property investors might wish to monitor how these expansion plans develop, particularly regarding the types of investment opportunities that may become available through the firm's master agent partnerships. As always, thorough research and independent financial advice remain crucial when considering any property investment.

While Elite Realty Invest's growth ambitions reflect confidence in the property market, market conditions can change rapidly. Investors may find it valuable to keep abreast of both domestic and international property trends when making long-term investment decisions.

item.title: 10% rise in sales agreed as housing recovers
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK housing market has shown signs of renewed activity, with a notable 10% increase in agreed sales during April compared to previous months. Industry analysts suggest this uptick may signal the beginning of a broader market recovery, with more accurately priced properties attracting buyer interest across Britain.

Improved pricing strategies boost market activity

Recent data indicates that sellers who have adjusted their pricing expectations in line with current market conditions are seeing greater success. Estate agents report that properties priced realistically from the outset are attracting more viewings and achieving faster sales. This shift towards pricing accuracy appears to be helping to bridge the gap between buyer expectations and seller aspirations.

Several factors may be contributing to this positive trend:

  • Mortgage rates showing signs of stabilisation after recent volatility
  • Increased buyer confidence as the economic outlook improves
  • Seasonal factors typically boosting spring market activity

Regional variations in market performance

Whilst the national picture shows improvement, regional variations remain significant. Areas with stronger employment markets and good transport links continue to outperform more peripheral locations. The Midlands and certain parts of the North West appear to be leading the recovery, with London's market showing more modest gains.

Prospective buyers might consider that properties in popular commuter towns and cities with good schools often maintain stronger demand. Meanwhile, sellers in less sought-after locations may need to be particularly mindful of competitive pricing strategies to attract interest.

The coming months will reveal whether this positive trend represents a sustained recovery or a temporary seasonal boost. For now, the increased sales activity offers cautious optimism for both buyers and sellers navigating the UK property market.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK housing market has shown signs of renewed activity, with a notable 10% increase in agreed sales during April compared to previous months. Industry analysts suggest this uptick may signal the beginning of a broader market recovery, with more accurately priced properties attracting buyer interest across Britain.

Improved pricing strategies boost market activity

Recent data indicates that sellers who have adjusted their pricing expectations in line with current market conditions are seeing greater success. Estate agents report that properties priced realistically from the outset are attracting more viewings and achieving faster sales. This shift towards pricing accuracy appears to be helping to bridge the gap between buyer expectations and seller aspirations.
